data.title: Nestle: Expected Returns Are Lower Than Bonds
data.description:
Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) (OTCPK:NSRGF) shares dropped 6% after reporting disappointing half-year results. Revenue continues to decline, organic growth is driven solely from pricing, and margins remain pressured.

It's hard to sugarcoat this. Even when looking at Nestlé's 'underlying' and 'organic' figures, almost all arrows are pointing down.

This leads to the question, what do bulls see here? I know what the bears see, that's for sure.

Let's dive in.

The Nestlé Bear Thesis

I've been covering Nestlé on Seeking Alpha since early 2023. Initially, I thought that it was an attractive low-risk investment, as I expected growth to accelerate after the post-pandemic trough.

Over time, it became clear to me that Nestlé's growth and margin issues aren't a story of tough comparisons and pandemic pullforward. In fact, it was a story of saturated markets and intrinsic limitations.

Simply put, companies that don't grow revenues and earnings have very few avenues to keep up with the market. And companies that trade at relatively high valuations ahead of a three-year stagnation are very likely to not even keep up with bonds.

That is, in a nutshell, the story of Nestlé of the past three years and why I remain bearish, even after the appointment of a new CEO.

I just think that the company's issues are structural, and unless a very aggressive CEO comes in and takes almost a private-equity-like approach to a turnaround, I continue to believe the stock will provide poor returns.

First Half Results Show Things Are Getting Worse, Not Better

In the first half of 2025, Nestlé sales were CHF 44.2 billion, down 1.8% Y/Y, with organic growth of 2.9% offset by a 4.7% FX headwind.

Gross margin contracted 60 bps, while underlying trading operating margin dropped 90 bps.

This has led to a 10% decline in net profit, a 9% decline in EPS, and a 42% drop in free cash flow.

If you're a glass-half-full kind of person, you might say that FX was the primary reason for the decline and that organic growth was decent. However, when unpacking the numbers, we note that pricing is once again the main driver of organic growth, while RIG (volume + mix) is back in the negative.

Looking at the different categories, it's notable that the main drivers of organic growth are Confectionery, and Powdered & Liquid Beverages, with the rest of the categories, except water, below total group numbers.

It's not a coincidence that the two categories saw the biggest margin declines, down 330 bps and 190 bps, respectively. High coffee and cocoa prices have led to significant price increases across the industry, but not enough to offset the cost pressures.

Looking at the rest of the categories, Water might be the only bright spot, with improving margins alongside positive RIG. Other than that, all categories are showing signs of struggle.

Looking For Signs Of Change Under New CEO

After going through the results above, it's hard for me to understand how the company opened its presentation with the following slide:

I mean, revenue dropped 3%, margins declined about a 100 bps, earnings were down 11%, and net debt surpassed CHF 60 billion primarily to fund buybacks that have been value destructive for years.

And yet, it was a good half?

As I wrote in the past, the first step in fixing and turning around a company is acknowledging the problems, and I don't think Nestlé is doing it.

They do have a plan for cost savings, but I would expect, if they were really ambitious, that we would have seen significant layoffs by now. So far, we can't see these savings in the margin, that's for sure.

They also have a plan to focus on their winning SKUs, but we've seen no divestitures since the new CEO appointment. In some sense, their talking about a possible divestment of certain vitamin units is perhaps the most positive sign in the whole presentation, if you ask me.

Expected Returns

If you read my articles before, this is a bit repetitive, but I think it's important to emphasize. My investing view is quite simple. If a company doesn't grow revenue and earnings faster than market average, the chances of it providing market-beating returns, or even market-like returns, are slim.

Nestlé is targeting, in the medium term, to achieve 4%+ organic sales growth, with the caveat of "in normal market conditions". It's also targeting a minor 50 bps underlying trading operating profit margin improvement. Needless to say, even if those targets are achieved, Nestlé won't be growing in line with the market.

Further, with the historical strength of the Swiss Frank, these adjusted figures are typically much better than reported numbers.

So, if you agree with the notion that growth is the main driver of expected returns, it's hard to justify investing in Nestlé, even if you believe it will execute on its plan.

Valuation

At $93 per ADR, or CHF 74 per Swiss-listed share, Nestlé trades around 17 to 18 times earnings.

On the surface, this isn't a stretched valuation. However, when adjusting for growth, it's still a rich valuation. I mean, how do you calculate a PEG (price to earnings to growth ratio) when earnings are declining?

And, again, even if they reach the medium-term targets, this is still trading at a 3x+ PEG.

Conclusion

Nestlé has a dominant position in a saturated market, with very few avenues to deliver consistent growth, let alone market-beating growth.

Investing in the stock is often justified by its supposed lower risk, dividend track record, or geographic diversification.

However, as we've demonstrated, those things did little to help it keep up with the returns of bonds, much less equity indexes.

So, I reiterate a 'Hold' rating and continue to encourage investors to allocate their money elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Realty Income: Best Time In 10 Years To Buy
data.description:
O stock: chart of the day

I last wrote on Realty Income (NYSE:O) stock on June 7. That article was titled “Realty Income Is Better Than Simon Property After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade”. It rated O as a buy after a comparative analysis against SPG. Since then, a few new developments call for a follow-up analysis on the stock. The rest of this article will concentrate on the top 2 developments on my radar: the stock’s latest dividend declarations and its upcoming FQ2 earnings report (ER).

Examining these new developments has led me to conclude that the current conditions surrounding the stock present the best time to buy the stock in at least 10 years, leading to a reiteration of my buy thesis. I will present various data points to support this thesis in the subsequent sections. But I think the chart that best encapsulates my argument is the one below. This chart illustrates the dividend yield spread of Realty Income relative to the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), used to approximate the overall REIT space in the past 10 years from 2016. As seen, over this period, the average spread has been 0.478%. The spread current is hovering around 1.712%, not only far above the historical average but also near the peak levels in 10 years, indicating O's unusually attractive valuation relative to the sector.

Next, I will explain why the attractiveness is even stronger given the latest dividend declarations and also the FQ2 earnings forecast.

O stock: latest dividend declarations

Realty Income is well known for its regular monthly dividends. Indeed, since my last writing, the company has declared a new dividend payout of $0.2690 per share for July and August, with an ex-dividend date of July 1 and August 1, respectively. This latest payout represents a 0.18% quarter-over-quarter increase from the $0.2685 paid from April through June 2025 and a 2.28% year-over-year increase from the same period in 2024.

Investors should not be under-impressed by the seemingly small growth rates. As illustrated above, its yield spread relative to VNQ is already near a 10-year peak on a TTM basis. With the latest payout raises—albeit small increases—the yield spread could be even thicker in terms of an FWD basis.

Further, as detailed next, the latest data also point to a lower payout ratio and thus more room for future dividend growth.

O stock: latest data suggest a lower FFO payout ratio

The company is set to announce its next earnings on August 6, 2025, with a normalized FFO estimate of $1.06 as seen in the next chart. Based on this data and also the monthly payout of $0.2685 per share in the second quarter of 2025, as shown above, the implied FFO payout ratio (on a normalized basis) for Q2 would be about 75.9%. As a background, the company reported a normalized FFO of $1.05 for Q1 of 2025. With the first three monthly payouts, the implied FFO payout ratio (again on a normalized basis) for Q1 is 75.8%.

These recent and FWD payout ratios are notably lower than their long-term averages, signaling more room for dividend growth in the years to come. To wit, the chart below displays Realty Income’s cash dividend payout ratio in the past decade, since 2016. The payout ratio has experienced some fluctuations over the years but has stayed in a relatively stable range of 72% and 88% most of the time, demonstrating the consistency of its business model. Overall, the average payout ratio is 79.00% in the past 10 years, and the FWD payout ratio I just estimated is toward the lower end of this historical spectrum.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of valuation, the stock is quite reasonably valued at a FWD P/FFO ratio of 13.6x in my view. But I think dividend yield is a more telling metric given its incredible track record of dividend growth. Along this line, the chart provided below shows Realty Income’s dividend yield in the past 10 years. The yield has stayed within a relatively stable range between around 3.00% and 6% most of the time, as seen, with an average of 4.46%. O's current TTM dividend yield stands at 5.49%, and the forward dividend yield is 5.56% based on the latest declaration. Both are again far above the historical average and close to the peak seen in the past 10 years.

On the negative side, O’s growth strategy relies heavily on acquisitions. Its debt had risen substantially in recent years, with long-term debt rising from about $15 billion in 2021 to the current level of almost $25 billion. As a reflection, its balance sheet has become more stretched since ~2021. As a reflection, the chart below shows its debt-to-equity ratio in the past 10 years. As seen, before 2021~2022, the ratio has remained below the 10-year average of 0.413 most of the time. There has been an upward trend in the debt-to-equity ratio, with values generally increasing since late 2021. Currently, O's debt-to-equity ratio stands at 0.518, not only far above its historical average but also near the highest leverage levels in the past decade. This is an area that I will closely monitor in its Q2 ER. A piece of good news on this front is that the chance of interest rate cuts has been increasing, as indicated by the data provided by the CME Group FedWatch Tool, which could help to lower O’s interest expense.

I started with a bigger picture by benchmarking O’s valuation against the overall REIT sector. To further drive the point, let me close with an even bigger picture by benchmarking the REIT sector’s valuation against other market sectors with the following dashboard (BTW, it is a Google sheet and you are welcome to download it). It’s a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) uses to monitor the macroscopic picture for our members. As seen, the REIT sector, as approximated by VNQ, is currently the second most attractively valued sector in terms of its yield Z-score, only after small caps.

With this, let me close by reiterating my buy thesis. To recap, the key developments examined here are the stock’s latest dividend declarations and its latest earnings revisions for the FQ2 ER. These developments have led me to see a usual valuation discount both by horizontal and vertical comparison, and also further dividend growth potential.

data.title: Parsons: Overcoming DOGE Scares
data.description:
In February, I called Parsons Corporation (PSN) a momentum and DOGE casualty. A combination of savvy M&A efforts and organic growth pushed up valuations of the technology-driven defense and infrastructure business a great deal in 2024. This left shares susceptible to a substantial pullback amidst slower growth and fears of the impact of the DOGE movement.

In fact, shares were cut in half, resetting the valuations to much more reasonable price levels, as investors feared an overhang of the emerging clouds on the 2025 results, and beyond. Growth has slowed down, yet the modest sales increase guided for 2025 provides a foundation under the shares, combined with bolt-on M&A and buybacks.

Stabilization has been found again, allowing shares to obtain a modest premium once more, leaving shares trading at neutral levels in my view.

Technology Solutions For Infra, Intelligence, And Defense

Parsons provides innovative solutions that make the world safer, healthier and more connected. The business has a corporate history going back 80 years, employs over 18,000 workers, and generates over $6 billion in sales. These revenues are split up across two main segments: a federal solutions business and a critical infrastructure business, with the former being slightly larger than the latter.

These segments are comprised of business units that include engineered systems, defense & intelligence, and infrastructure serving markets within transportation, space, cyber & intelligence, infrastructure protection, environmental remediation and urban development. The business is 75% focused on the US, complemented by operations in Canada and the MEA region.

Most contracts are so-called prime contracts, with just a third being fixed price in terms of nature, the remainder based on time and materials or costs reimbursed.

Amidst the combination of organic growth on M&A efforts, the business has seen strong growth, having comfortably doubled sales over the past decade from $3 billion in 2016 to more than $6 billion now, although that sales were stuck around the $4 billion mark for quite a while after the public offering, with momentum only really seen in recent years, driven by increased political tensions.

Moreover, low-single digit operating margins have expanded towards high single digits with the passage of time, amidst a manageable dilution of the shareholder base incurred.

The business has been public since 2019 after they made the public debut at $27 per share, as all the way through the year 2023, shares have been trending around the $40 mark, before political tensions and accelerated growth triggered a huge momentum move higher in 2023 and certainly in 2024, with shares peaking at $115 per share.

This was followed by a violent pullback to the $60 mark early this year, with shares nearly cut in half, as shares have now recovered alongside the wider market to current levels around $75 per share.

The Momentum Run

After flattish results immediately after the pandemic, momentum was really ignited in 2023 with sales up 30% to $5.4 billion, largely driven by organic growth. The company guided for 2024 sales initially at $5.9 billion, as increased momentum and a $200 million deal for BlackSingal Technologies in the summer of last year, added to the fire.

Of course, this was reflected in the share price which revealed huge momentum, after which shares came crashing down to $60 in February, when I last took a look at the shares. Just before, the company reported 2024 sales up 24% to $6.8 billion, with growth largely driven by organic growth. Operating profits of $428 million resulted in GAAP earnings of $235 million, equal to $2.12 per share. Adjusted earnings came in at $3.26 per share, as I pegged realistic earnings close to $2.75 per share, which is after backing out stock-based compensation expenses.

The company called for modest sales growth in 2025, seeing sales come in between $7.0-$7.5 billion, with EBITDA seen up towards $640-$710 million, compared to a $605 million number in 2024. With realistic earnings seen topping $3 per share, valuation multiples compressed towards 20 times earnings. Given the background of the business, it looked reasonably compelling, although the full-year backlog rose by just 3% to $8.9 billion.

At those levels, valuations looked much more reasonable in my view, yet there were still real concerns about DOGE and government budgets, and even as shares have lost half their value, they were still up compared to the years before. However, I feared that DOGE concerns might trigger even better entry opportunities.

A Modest Recovery

Since February, shares of Parsons were not really hurt by concerns about tariffs, yet this hardly impacted the stock given the nature of the business and the fact that shares had come down so much beforehand already. In a gradual fashion, shares have recovered to the $75 mark here.

As a sign of confidence and signaling cheaper valuations, the board of Parsons announced a sizable $250 million share buyback program in March, making a dent in relation to the current $8 billion market capitalization at $75 per share. Of course, shares were trending at lower levels when this program was announced, as a $60 share price looked quite reasonable and compelling, as I like the timing of the announcement of the capital allocation move (which is not necessarily the same as execution of buybacks, of course).

By the end of April, Parsons reported a 1% increase in first quarter sales to $1.6 billion, with organic sales down 2%. EBITDA rose by 5% to $149 million, with GAAP earnings up over 60% to $0.60 per share. A book-to-bill ratio came in at 1.1 times again, pushing up the backlog to $9.1 billion. Net debt was reported at $950 million, with this debt level increasing following poor cash flow generation and some early buybacks, with the buyback program announced the week before the quarter ended.

With 111 million shares outstanding (assuming dilution) trading at $75 per share) the market value comes to around $8.3 billion and the enterprise valuation is about $9.2 billion. This values the business at about 1.3-1.4 times sales and 13–14 times EBITDA.

Adding Inorganic Growth

On the first day of July, Parsons announced its next bolt-on deal, as the company announced that it will acquire Chesapeake Technologies in an $89 million deal.

CTI adds defense customer posture, adding additional capabilities as a technology solutions provider with products offered to improve the ability to participate in the invisible battlefield. The transaction involves more than 200 workers being added to Parsons and is valued at exactly 1 times sales, with the revenue contribution seen at par compared to the purchase price.

The deal is valued at roughly 10 times EBITDA, with both multiples being quite reasonable. In reality this is just over a bolt-on deal, with the pro forma sales contribution seen at around 1.3%, with the EBITDA contribution seen a little bit higher, but in reality this is really a bolt-on transaction.

Pro forma net debt will jump to just over a billion, but likely higher amidst the announced buyback program. Given the anticipated improvements for the year, I see pro forma earnings over $3 per share, perhaps closer to $3.25 per share on a realistic basis. However, shares have recovered some 25% from the DOGE-inspired lows, pushing up the earnings multiples to about 23 times here, which makes a small premium is reappearing again, amidst negative organic growth.

All this makes the shares trade at neutral levels, as I look forward to updating from here and buying potential renewed dips, but for now, I am not yet willing to consider the shares here.

data.title: Opendoor: One Of Many Meme Stocks That Could Go Under
data.description:
Opendoor Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:OPEN) appears to have created an innovative approach to home buying and selling. You sell your home to them quickly with no showings or hassle. They buy it from you, do some repairs, and flip it. It sounds wise to use data and scale to make transactions more efficient, and if home prices climb consistently over time, as they tend to, it’s an additional profit bonus for the inventory holder. But the model has major flaws, and it has become obvious that the company is a cash incinerator that I believe will fail if funding dries up.

The company can’t turn a profit. It loses hundreds of millions yearly and $100 million quarterly, even after slashing costs in the past few years. In Q1 2025, they posted lower sales, gross profit, and gross margins YoY, all while reducing operating expenses.

Their algorithm attempts to predict local home values (but appears to provide little value in that regard) and turn over inventory fast at low margins. They likely attempt to mitigate pricing risk through quick transaction times, but they won’t be able to mitigate this risk as inventory keeps spiking and nationwide transactions keep drying up, and as it continues, they hold an expensive and illiquid collection of assets.

The business model has shown an inability to be profitable through a period of rapidly rising home values, and they’re now exposed to what looks increasingly like a housing bubble that has been very slowly deflating since the peak in late 2022, after soaring 40% from 2020. That spike is similar in magnitude to the rise from 2002–2007, and median home prices and household incomes are higher now than at the 2007 peak, as you can see in the chart below. The difference this time is that much of the excess was driven by artificially low mortgage rates, which have since reversed sharply. Now, with affordability at record lows, inventory starting to rise, and rates stuck above 6%, prices will remain under serious pressure, and selling housing inventory for a profit will likely become much more difficult.

The chart above makes clear the growing gap between U.S. median household income and median home prices since 2017. While incomes have risen modestly over two decades, home prices have surged disproportionately—especially in the last five years. Affordability has deteriorated and current home valuations are likely unsustainable.

Now that mortgage rates are far higher than in the 2013-2020 period, home transactions are plummeting with rising inventory, and Opendoor is stuck with expensive homes that carry interest, taxes, wear and tear. Opendoor finances its inventory with debt, and higher rates significantly increase the cost of holding unsold homes. It now takes longer to sell homes, buyers face higher mortgage payments, and demand is dropping. This reduces the margins Opendoor has experienced historically, which are difficult enough to work with when gross margin is in the single digits.

This matters because the business is a leveraged bet on home prices continuing to rise or at least staying flat. If housing undergoes a prolonged correction, and there are signs it already is in many markets (many cities are still -10-20% lower than their 2022 pricing peak), Opendoor likely fails. It likely doesn’t have the pricing power or speed to survive long stretches of price declines and/or low nationwide sales of housing inventory. The company will likely sell fewer homes at lower prices, as it absorbs real losses on each transaction while carrying the financing burden of unsold inventory. In an enduring falling market, this becomes fatal, considering their debt load and operating losses. With approximately $1.7 billion in net debt to service and annual interest payments of around $130 million, the bottom line will have to improve dramatically and quickly to keep going without further funding. They don't have the luxury of holding inventory for long.

Given these risks, I see Opendoor’s valuation as frankly far too high, and I know that sounds absurd since it currently trades at half of sales (in a bear market, it could get far cheaper even if it manages to survive), but what are we buying? Despite the losses and a questionable path to success, it’s been swept up in 2021-style short-buying. Retail is gambling as usual, with little justification other than a high percentage of the float shorted, a former high price, and a current low share price (rarely do fundamentals factor into the decision-making, as nothing fails anymore, and every dip is rewarded in this market). Nothing about the underlying business justifies the spike, although that’s not unique in this stock market (as Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has shown us). When the meme-mania dies, gamblers will be left holding shares in a company that has never turned a profit, operates with razor-thin margins, and could easily collapse in a prolonged housing downturn. Investors may tolerate years of unprofitability for a high-growth tech firm with sticky software revenue, but likely not for a home flipper that depends on timing local housing markets. The major caveat here is that they could do a large issuance to retail during the meme-spike as Gamestop did to raise cash, which could help the firm survive a few more years.

Unless there's a drastic shift in the housing market or a total reinvention of its business model, Opendoor will have trouble surviving, in my opinion. I believe the stock is exposed to falling home prices, rising rates, declining buyer demand, and the consequences of one of the largest housing bubbles in U.S. history. The model appeared innovative in a rising market, but in a stagnant one, they are just one of many companies trying to dump expensive housing inventory.

data.title: Ameris Bancorp: A Respectful Downgrade After A Great Run
data.description:
Things have gone very well for shareholders of Ameris Bancorp (ABCB) since I rated the company a "Buy" in October 2023. At the time, I recognized that there were some negative aspects of the company. But even so, I believed that the small bank offered an attractive upside. Little did I know just how strong that upside would end up being. You see, whenever I rate a company a "Buy," I'm making the claim that shares are likely to outperform the market for the foreseeable future. And outperform shares did! Since my bullish assessment of the company, the stock has appreciated 77.4%. That's significantly above the 47.2% increase the S&P 500 saw over the same period.

Looking at the picture today, I see a firm that remains cheap relative to other similar enterprises. Asset quality is mixed, but slightly positive. But honestly, the absolute valuation of the company is a bit higher than I would like it to be. Because of this, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes sense. However, my opinion could change in the coming days as management is due to announce financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on July 28. The current expectation is for revenue and earnings per share to increase year over year. But that alone, unless it is by a significant margin, will not be enough to change my decision here.

Still solid, but there are better opportunities

For the most part, the fundamental picture for Ameris Bancorp has been positive. For instance, deposits for the institution continue to expand. From 2023 to 2024, they grew from $20.71 billion to $21.72 billion. They increased further to $21.91 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Unfortunately, a lot of this recent increase can be attributed to a rise in brokered deposits. These grew from $804.9 million at the end of last year to $1.05 billion in the first quarter. Brokered deposits are not inherently bad, but they are more costly and not always as reliable as traditional deposits. So that is something for investors to take into consideration. It's also worth mentioning that uninsured deposit exposure amounted to $10.01 billion in the most recent quarter. This represents 45.7% of overall deposits and is well above the 30% maximum that I typically like to see. When shares of a bank are heavily discounted as the firm was in the past, that is more acceptable. But today, this is not the case.

Investors should also pay attention to the value of loans. These came out to $20.36 billion in the most recent quarter. This is down from the $20.40 billion the company had at the end of last year. The largest exposure that the company has in its loan portfolio would be to commercial and farmland real estate. $8.57 billion, or approximately 41.4%, of its overall loan portfolio is tied up in these types of assets. Having said that, the enterprise also has a lot of exposure to residential real estate, commercial and industrial loans, construction and development loans, and even the premium finance space.

The last item here, premium finance loans, might seem a bit foreign. This would be the case even if you're familiar with investing in banks. This is a niche loan category that involves providing loans for the acquisition of commercial insurance policies. As part of the agreements that it has with borrowers, Ameris Bancorp even has the ability to cancel insurance policies after default by the borrower so that they can minimize the risk of loss moving forward. This is certainly interesting to me.

While the value of loans has been declining, the value of securities has been on the rise. These grew from about $1.62 billion in 2023 to $1.90 billion in 2024. There was a further increase to $2.18 billion in the first quarter of this year. At the same time, cash and cash equivalents have been rising, with an increase from $1.17 billion in 2023 to $1.29 billion today. Simultaneously, management has been successful in reducing the overall debt. Over this period, debt has fallen from $639.9 million to $409.6 million.

So far, I would say that from a fundamental perspective, things are looking up. When we look at the income statement, we see a continuation of this positive situation. From 2023 to 2024, net interest income for the company rose from $692.4 million to $790.4 million. This came even as the net interest margin for the business contracted from 3.61% to 3.56%. And it was due to the increase in the size of the balance sheet, combined with a meaningful drop in the provision for credit losses for the company from $142.7 million to $58.8 million. Continued growth for the company, and a jump in the net interest margin from 3.51% to 3.73%, allowed its net interest margin in the first quarter of this year to total $199.9 million. That's up from the $180.3 million reported for last year.

Non-interest income has been far more range-bound. After jumping from $242.8 million in 2023 to $293.3 million last year, with a lot of that upside coming from a surge in mortgage banking activity from $139.9 million to $160.5 million, the company reported a slight drop to $64 million in the first quarter this year compared to the $65.9 million reported the previous year. Even with this being the case, net profits have continued to increase. From 2023 to 2024, they popped from $269.1 million to $358.7 million. And from the first quarter of last year to the first quarter of this year, they rose from $74.3 million to $87.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using historical results from 2024. The price-to-earnings multiple that the company is trading at comes in at 12.9, which is unfortunately above the 10 maximum that I like to see in this industry. But on a relative basis, the firm is cheaper than four of the five companies I compared it to in that chart. Those firms are ServisFirst Bancshares, Inc. (SFBS), Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB), BancFirst Corporation (BANF), Axos Financial, Inc. (AX), and Flagstar Financial, Inc. (FLG). In the chart below, I then looked at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. Once again, Ameris Bancorp was cheaper than four of the five companies I compared it to. But with these multiples coming in a bit above the 1 that I like to see, this certainly tilts the business more in the direction of being fairly valued.

Honestly, the absolute trading multiples of the business are not terrible. But for me to take a bullish stance on the firm, I would need to see that the asset quality was higher than it is. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the return on assets of the same five enterprises I compared it to already. With a reading of 1.36%, Ameris Bancorp is comfortably above the 1% minimum that I like to see. And it's around the middle of the group compared to these other players. In the subsequent chart, the return on equity reading that we get of 9.39% is almost at the 10% minimum that I like. It's close enough that I am more open to the company, but only to the extent that shares would be undervalued instead of fairly valued.

It's possible that my overall assessment could change as new data comes out. And it just so happens that, after the market closes on July 28, management will be releasing results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that time, analysts believe that net revenue will be $298.9 million. This would be above the $281.9 million the company reported at the same time in 2024. Meanwhile, earnings per share are expected to climb from $1.32 to $1.33, taking net profits from $90.8 million to $91.8 million. That's not bad, but it's unlikely to be enough to change my overall assessment absent other material changes for the business.

Takeaway

At this time, I still think Ameris Bancorp is a fine business. But honestly, I don't think it's cheap enough, and I don't think asset quality is quite high enough to justify a bullish outlook. Instead, I would argue that the company justifies a respectful downgrade from a "Buy" to a "Hold." But it wouldn't take much for me to consider upgrading it to a "Buy" once again.

data.title: Tesla: Why I Am Willing To Continue To Pay The 'Tesla Premium'
data.description:
The stock price of Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) fell 8% on Thursday in response to the electric-vehicle company reporting results for its second quarter. Tesla’s earning presentation for Q2 '25 showed a substantial decrease in sales and operating profits compared to the second quarter of last year, as consumer boycotts unfolded and delivery volumes crashed in the first six months of the year.

The decrease in sales and profits highlights increased risk for investors and could possibly indicate that the stock of Tesla will be vulnerable in the short-term. However, Tesla still booked solid operating profits of more than $900 million and second quarter deliveries increased significantly on a QoQ basis, suggesting that the electric-vehicle company has moved past the most severe phase of consumer boycotts.

My last stop classification on Tesla was Buy, predominantly because no other company in the United States was pumping out a higher volume of electric vehicles. I think that Tesla remains compelling as a investment vehicle for investors that want to bet on the transition to electric vehicles long-term.

Tesla’s Earnings Disappoint, Experiences Operating Margin Pressure

Tesla’s quarterly non-GAAP profits fell to $0.40 per share, down 23% YoY as the electric-vehicle company dealt with the fallout from a broad consumer strike triggered by CEO Elon Musk’s involvement in politics during the last U.S. presidential election. Sales fell 12% YoY to $22.50 billion while the consensus called for sales of $22.74 billion.

Moreover, Tesla suffered a 16% YoY decrease in its automotive sales to $16.7 billion, with the drop in sales catalyzed by falling deliveries. Consequently, Tesla also suffered a not-insignificant drop in its operating profits. Tesla’s 2Q25 operating profit fell 42% YoY to $923 million whereas its operating profit margin clocked in at 4.1%, reflecting a 2.19 percentage point decrease compared to the second quarter last year.

More margin pressure may unfold for the electric-vehicle company if delivery volumes don’t rebound in the third and fourth quarters. While automotive sales decreased YoY, they increased 19% QoQ as delivery volumes rebounded.

And here’s an overview of Tesla’s sales and operating profit trajectory.

Tesla’s decrease in deliveries has been substantial based on absolute figures, but should be put into perspective as well in order to remain fair to the EV company.

According to Cox Automotive, Tesla saw the biggest absolute drop in deliveries in the entire market in the first half of the year, but Tesla also has a much higher delivery volume than other EV companies.

For instance, General Motors Company (GM) delivered 78,000 electric-vehicles to customers in the first six months of 2025, but Tesla, being the volume-leader that it is, delivered almost 721K EVs which is still substantially more, despite slumping sales.

Tesla delivered 384,122 electric-vehicles to customers in the second quarter, well below Wall Street's expectation of 389,407 EVs and down 13% YoY. As mentioned above, Tesla’s deliveries recovered on a QoQ, however, by more than 47K EVs. The total delivery record still benefits Tesla, and I think that the electric vehicle company deserves the benefit of the doubt as long as its operating profits and margins are positive.

Elon Musk said that he expects headwinds in the short term amid a number of challenges, including a delay to Tesla’s robotaxi service. That being said, Tesla’s delivery drop in 2Q25 was really not that severe and the electric-vehicle company managed to remain profitable on both an operating and net income level in the second quarter.

Over the long haul, Elon Musk’s ability to forecast trends ahead of time, enter new markets and develop new sales streams has been extraordinary. It is the main reason why the stock is selling at a premium relative to other EV manufacturers.

Why I Am Still Willing To Pay A Juicy ‘Tesla Premium’

The biggest criticism that I receive for having a bullish view on electric-vehicle company Tesla is that the stock seems outrageously expensive, particularly taking into account the recent slump in electric-vehicle deliveries.

With a leading Price-To-Sales ratio of 8.7x, it should be clear that Tesla is not a bargain, particularly with the stock of Rivian Automotive Inc. (RIVN), for instance, selling for a Price-To-Sales Ratio of only 2.2x. Rivian Automotive reset its production and delivery guidance earlier this year, underscoring that the EV company is also facing delivery and sales risks. Tesla, however, is the biggest EV brand in the United States and as such uniquely positioned to play an important role in the robotaxi market as well.

Not that long ago, Tesla’s stock changed hands for $488, a record high at the time, and I would not be surprised in the least if the EV company were to retest these highs in the next 12-18 months on the back of a robotaxi service start as well as a more fervent push into autonomous vehicles, a big market for Tesla and other EV companies.

Based on estimates from market.us, the autonomous vehicle market could grow to a volume of $3.1 trillion by 2033, suggesting that Tesla could add a new sales stream to its business.

Lucid Group (LCID) is anticipated to see the biggest jump in sales next year (101%), and the stock is also considerably cheaper than Tesla’s, at 3.3x 2026e sales (though not as cheap as Rivian Automotive). Lucid Motors also jumped head-first in the robotaxi opportunity, linking up with ride-sharing giant Uber Inc. (UBER) and tech startup Nuro.

I think that the Tesla premium, taking specifically into account Tesla’s record of building a globally-operating EV brand, is justified. I would also add that Tesla recently had considerable success in China with the Model Y becoming the best-selling passenger car in the country. Tesla’s June sales were up 4% YoY ,and the EV company sold 61,484 vehicles in China that month. They were led by Tesla’s Model Y, the second-best result since March 2025.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be Challenged

A bullish investment thesis for Tesla will undoubtedly be attacked energetically as the stock has become divisive and a volleyball in politics.

That being said, though, Tesla is still the number one-ranked EV brand in the United States, and the EV company is seeing robust success with its Model Y in China. This paints a more differentiated picture than the singular focus on Tesla’s deliveries.

Put simply, if Elon Musk avoids stays out of the political limelight and EV deliveries continue to recover moving forward, I think that Tesla has a good chance for an upside stock retracement and could ultimately also retest its all-time highs.

My Takeaway

Tesla’s stock slumped 8% on Thursday, a day after the electric-vehicle company made its second quarter earnings presentation. Though automotive sales fell 16% YoY and operating margins also contracted compared to last year, operating profits were still positive.

Delivery volumes also recovered QoQ (and so did sales and operating profits), potentially underscoring that investors have gotten carried away with their negative attitudes toward the EV company.

Model Y sales in China were great in June and with less political involvement on the horizon for Elon Musk, there is a chance that a rational assessment of Tesla’s prospects could come to foreground again.

With all of that said, I think that the "Tesla premium" is still worth paying. Buy.

data.title: Brunswick Corporation: Shifting My View To More Neutral Given The Green Shoots
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Brunswick Corporation (NYSE:BC) was a sell rating because I expected the poor macro backdrop, plus the tariff situation, to heavily impact demand, and that would directly impact earnings. While margin pressure remains a major issue, there are now visible green shoots in retail demand and strong execution on tariff mitigation. Propulsion outperformance and potential advantages from domestic manufacturing are also encouraging. That said, execution risks are still high, especially with the back-loaded FY25 guide. Given this more balanced setup, I now move to a hold.

2Q25 results update

BC’s 2Q25 adj. EPS did slightly better than consensus, but if you look at the y/y comparison, it still paints a tough picture. Net sales grew 0.2% y/y to $1.447 billion, which, to be fair, is better than the mid-teens decline that consensus was expecting. That support came almost entirely from the Propulsion segment, which grew 7.2% y/y against a strong US OEM demand. The Parts & Accessories segment also held steady, up 0.5% y/y. That said, these gains were offset by a 6.6% y/y decline in boat sales, as dealers remained cautious, and a 3.7% drop at Navico. While the top line held flat, profitability took a hit. Adj. EBIT margin fell 380 bps y/y to 8.7%, and that pulled adj. EPS down 35.6% y/y to $1.16.

Emerging Bright Spots Offer Cautious Optimism

This quarter gave the first real sign that demand may be starting to stabilize. After a soft start in 2Q25, management said that internal retail sales trends improved meaningfully from June into July. Retail activity for boats, engines, and P&A turned positive y/y in July. This is perhaps the best news for a shareholder of BC in a long while. While it’s too early to call this an inflection, this uptick suggests consumer interest is not dead and, if sustained, could support a pickup in wholesale orders in 2H25. In particular, propulsion performance was a huge surprise to me. Per management, Mercury gained more than 300 bps of US retail share in high-horsepower outboards and 30 bps overall on a 12-month basis. Given that this was achieved in a down market, I thought it was a strong signal of BC’s brand strength.

The other important update is on tariffs. In my update in April, I highlighted tariffs as a major earnings risk. Again, BC surprised me here in how well they have executed by shifting suppliers, staging inventory, and optimizing facilities. It was done so well that management revised their 2025 net tariff headwind down to $55–$70 million from the previous $100–$125 million estimate. That’s a huge reduction and materially de-risked FY25 earnings outlook. The other surprise was how BC actually became a partial beneficiary of this tariff situation. Specifically, the new tariff structure is now hitting competitors’ imported engines—while BC, with its mostly domestic manufacturing footprint, may actually be positioned to gain share. If BC continues to position itself well (reducing its exposure to tariffs), it could use this situation to gain more share, putting it in a better spot to ride the next upcycle.

Persistent Headwinds and Execution Risks Remain

Now, moving on to the negative takeaways that offset the positive developments noted above. Firstly, 2Q25 results once again showed that BC is not generating enough revenue to maintain its margin. Adj. EBIT margin declined another 380 bps y/y, with sharp drops in both Propulsion (-420 bps) and Boat (-290 bps). It is worse than it looked because BC actually generated flat revenue y/y, which in normal circumstances should mean a similar margin profile. But no, this tells us that BC cannot grow topline as fast as cost inflation, or there is a significant negative mix shift impact going on. I doubt it's due to the latter since high-horsepower outboards saw positive traction (this category carries a higher margin). The result is steep decremental margins that are likely to persist since demand is unlikely to see a sharp V-shaped recovery given the current macro backdrop. Management's FY25 guidance also implies no quick margin recovery.

Next, it relates to my earlier concern about weakness in the entry-level segment. Nothing has really improved here. Management disclosed that retail sales for its value boat brands were down ~20% y/y in 1H25, and I don’t see this situation turning around anytime soon, since these customers are very likely having affordability issues. As a result, BC is rationalizing 25% of its value fiberglass lineup for the 2026 model year—this process involves taking out the models that are selling the least in order to reduce excess complexity in a market that has become smaller. As much as that is the “right” strategy to approach this situation, it is also clear evidence that a demand recovery remains out of sight, particularly at the lower end of the market.

Lastly, the expectations that management has set for the rest of this year are also not something I appreciate. They guided for 3Q25 adj. EPS to $0.75–$0.90, which implies 9M25 adj. EPS of ~$2.60. This implies a 4Q25 adj. EPS of ~$0.65 to meet the midpoint (~$3.25) of FY25 guidance. On a y/y basis, this implies adj. EPS growth of 174%, and to achieve that, revenue has to grow by ~25% (assuming 4Q25 net margin is the same as 1H25). This is a huge ramp-up in revenue growth that, even if July retail trends hold, they would need to really accelerate hard through the rest of the year, which feels ambitious given the macro backdrop.

Valuation

I am still not bullish on BC despite the positive developments. I believe those were positive green shoots, but I doubt we will see a strong recovery anytime soon. Moreover, the implied recovery for 4Q25 seems too ambitious to me. In contrast to my belief, the market is clearly more optimistic, as BC valuation has gone up to 9x forward EBITDA, a 1.2x increase from 7.8x when I gave an update in April. I don’t think BC deserves to trade at 9x because of the headwinds I mentioned above, but looking at how Trump has started to conclude trade deals with other nations and the green shoots showing demand interest are not dead yet, the slight possibility of BC seeing a strong demand should not be ignored. And for that, I am shifting away from a sell rating to hold.

Conclusion

My view for BC is a hold rating. While margin pressure and entry-level weakness remain unresolved, the improving retail trends in July, better-than-expected tariff mitigation, and Propulsion segment strength are green shoots. The stock now trades at 9x forward EBITDA, which feels rich given execution risks, but with trade tailwinds potentially easing and consumer interest showing signs of life, I can’t rule out a stronger rebound. Hence, I think staying neutral is the right call.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Despite trade wars, battlefield wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, declining earnings estimates and a deteriorating fiscal outlook, the S&P 500 Index rose 10.9% in the second quarter and is up 6.2% at the year’s halfway mark.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the energy and IT sectors was positive.
  • Detractors: An underweight to IT and an overweight to energy, as well as stock selection in the communication services, health care and industrials sectors weighed on relative results.
  • Outlook: It seems to us that risks abound, yet asset prices do not reflect this. We continue to emphasize a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies compounding their dividends at attractive returns.

Performance Review

  • Despite trade wars, battlefield wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, declining earnings estimates and a deteriorating fiscal outlook, the S&P 500 Index is up 6.2% at the year’s halfway mark. Growth trounced value in the second quarter, led by Nvidia, which surged over 40%. The ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Fund (MUTF:SOPAX)(MUTF:SOPYX), with its emphasis on quality, risk management and a disciplined approach to valuation, lagged in this momentum-driven tape.
  • An overweight to energy and underweight to IT were the largest detractors in terms of sector attribution. Within these sectors positioning in natural gas–focused Enbridge (ENB), EQT (EQT) and Williams (WMB)(in energy) was positive, while in IT an underweight to the underperforming Apple contributed positively along with active positions in Oracle (ORCL) and Broadcom (AVGO).
  • Oracle beat estimates in its latest earnings report and raised its guidance, citing sizable acceleration in its cloud infrastructure revenues and growth expected to exceed that of hyperscaler peers.
  • Renewed enthusiasm for AI buildouts has benefited Broadcom tremendously as its custom-designed chips have continued to gain broader adoption with hyperscalers as an alternative to Nvidia’s (NVDA) dominant graphics processing units in AI applications.
  • Medical devices, laboratory equipment and diagnostic products maker Becton, Dickinson (BDX) reported marginally slower than expected organic sales growth across its portfolio, in part driven by headwinds from its China operations and by a lower global research funding environment for its biosciences division; a slight reduction in expected earnings resulted in a dramatic multiple contraction.
  • UnitedHealth Group (UNH) saw a renewed selloff in May following a first-quarter earnings miss and guidance reduction as the company announced even further elevated cost pressure and the exit of the managed care company’s CEO Andrew Witty.

Outlook

  • It seems to us that risks abound, yet asset prices do not reflect this. We continue to emphasize a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies compounding their dividends at attractive returns. Touting diversification may sound trite, but in today’s market it is no small feat. While the market has become incredibly concentrated, we have maintained a more traditionally diversified portfolio. Over the last eight years, our average portfolio company has grown its dividend by 7%, well in excess of inflation, and we see no reason that cannot continue. As inflation expectations become more entrenched and stagflation becomes a concern, dividend growth will become increasingly critical. Dividends act as an anchor to windward, stabilizing our portfolio during bouts of volatility. With elevated uncertainty likely an enduring feature of the current era, the course we chart should serve us well.

A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance 1gures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Real estate investment trusts (REITS) are closely linked to the performance of the real estate markets. REITs are subject to illiquidity, credit and interest rate risks, and risks associated with small- and mid-cap investments. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. Short selling is a speculative strategy. Unlike the possible loss on a security that is purchased, there is no limit on the amount of loss on an appreciating security that is sold short. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Dividends may fluctuate and are not guaranteed, and a company may reduce or eliminate its dividend at any time. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Stagflation is a seemingly contradictory condition described by slow economic growth and relatively high unemployment, or economic stagnation, which is at the same time accompanied by rising prices (i.e. inflation).

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 06/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 3 and 5 and fund’s Class I shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 5 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Class A and Class I shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.
